rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical anti-Markovnikov azidoacetamidation of styrenes with TMSN3 and MeCN to give vicinal azidoacetamides via a β-azido Ritter intermediate
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the styrene to a radical cation, followed by a second anodic oxidation of the β-azido benzylic radical to a benzyl cation

key_intermediates.name: β-azido benzyl alcohol from water trapping (E)

electron_flow: The anode oxidizes styrene to a radical cation, which is trapped by TMSN3 to give a β-azido benzylic radical; a second anodic oxidation generates a benzyl cation that is intercepted by MeCN to form a Ritter nitrilium, and hydrolysis delivers the vicinal azidoacetamide. Protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

probe_reagents.name: vinylcyclopropane radical clock (substrate 30)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened allyl-azide/acetamide product 31 obtained in 17% (4.4:1), consistent with a carbon radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: chemical oxidants (NFSI, TBHP, CAN, m-CPBA, DDQ, PhI(OAc)2, AgNO3)
probe_reagents.observation: None reproduced the anodic reaction (product n.d. except trace); only the anode gave 56% of 10, supporting a specifically electrochemical oxidation pathway

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of styrene, TMSN3, and product 3
probe_reagents.observation: Styrene Ep/2 = 1.92 V is oxidized preferentially over TMSN3 (no oxidation peak); product 3 (Ep/2 = 0.95 V) is more easily oxidized than starting material, explaining overoxidation/lower yields

notes: A competing pathway diverts the benzyl cation (C) to a β-azido alcohol (E) via water trapping instead of MeCN; this is the main off-pathway side product. The CV result (alkene oxidized, not azide) contradicts the authors' earlier HFIP-mediated azidotetrazolation work where TMSN3 was oxidized first, indicating a switch from azide-radical addition to alkene-radical-cation initiation under these conditions.
